THE SILVER STATE.
Friday .l>< «-eml»<-r is, ix*r>.
FLA1I.M; A DKNAIiOMC.
Some months ago the Reno Gazette,
which was doubtless inspired by corpor
ation influences in San Francisco, an
nounced that William M. Stewart, who
has not been actually nor constructively
a citizen of Nevada since the expiration
of his Senatorial term in 1873, would be
a candidate for United States Senator
from this State to succeed Senator Fair.
Subsequent to this announcement Mr.
Stewart made occasional visits to Neva
da, and published a monograph on silver
in the Enterprise, which theSiLVERSTATE
learned from a reliable source was the
joint production of himself and G. W.
Baker of Eureka. In this monograph
he asserted that silver was secretly and
fraudulently demonetized in 1873 by
surreptitiously inserting a section to
that effect in the Revised Statutes.
The Silver State expressed the belief
that Mr. Stewart, while Senator from
this State, voted for the bill to demon
etize silver in 1873, but not having the
Congressional Globe of that date at
hand it coaid not positively assert that
he did so.
Now General R. PlartA in iLa
Reno Journal shows that Stewart is a
blatant demagogue, and his “mono
graph ” a tissue of falsehoods. He
shows from the record that there is not
a word or syllable on the subject of sil
ver in the Revised Statutes that is not
contained in the Act of February 12,
1873; that the attention of both Houses
of Congress was repeatedly called to
the subject; that the bill was thoroughly
debated; and its provisions explained in
detail and well understood by the mem
bers of both Houses; that Mr. Stewart
himself both spoke to and voted for the
bill; that Mr. Stewart's remarks are re
corded in the Congressional Globe for
1870-71, on pages 371. 372, 373, 374,
377, 390 and 397, and that it passed the
Senate January 10, 1871, Mr. Stewart
voting for the bill. The bill was amend
ed in the House, and returned to the
Senate in May, 1872, and discussed at
length, Mr. Stewart participating in the
debates, and the Senate not concurring
in the House amendments, the bill was
referred to a conference committee of
the two Houses and finally became a
law in February, 1873, nearly three
years after its introduction.
General Clarke literally flays the
demagogue alive, and shows that while
Senator from Nevada he did not con
sider silver coin good enough for the
bondholders, which in true sand lot
fashion he now denounces, hoping to
gain popularity by doing so, but advo
cated by voice and vote the passage of
» bill demonetizing silver, by reduciug
the legal teuder quality of coins of that
metal to amounts not exceeding five
In the face of his record and falsehoods
on silver, no friend of that metal, be he
Republican or Democrat caQ support
William M. Stewart for United States
Senator from Nevada, even though he
should build a dozen houses in Carson
in which to reside at intervals until
after the ensuing election.
The funeral of General Robert
Toombs took place at Washington
Georgia, yesterday. He was oue of the
most popular mep in bis native State
and was never defeated in a political
contest before the people. He was au
unreconstructed rebel, and with Jeff.
Davis enjoyed the distinction of being
the only native-born white man iu the
United States, who was debarred from
the rights aud privileges of citizeuship.
Says Henry Clay Deau: “The Amer
ican gentleman sends to England for a
Durham to improve his cattle, to Franc*
for a Norman to improve his horses, to
Spain for a Merino to improve his
sheep, and turns around and marries
his daughter to a slim, empty-headed,
pesky little dude.
There are three counties aud three
towns iu Idaho of the same name —
Boise, Shoshone aud Idaho. Boise City
is not in Boise county, Shoshone is not
in Shoshone county, and Idaho City is
not in Idaho county.
The Virginia Legislature has elected
Johu W. Daniel Uuited States Sepatoi
to succeed Mahoue. The latter is dis
gusted, aud is going to emigrate to the
.West aud grow up with the country.
telegraphic news.
Boycotting by the Central Labor
Union of John Sherman, Secretary En
dicott, Warner Miller and others fcr
stopping at the Fifth Avenue Hotel,
New \ork city, has attracted some at
tention. Recently the proprietors of
the Fifth Avenue became involved in a
quarrel with the Printers’Labor Union,
and the latter association immediately
decided to boycott the hotel, and every
one who stopped there. For obvious
reasons the boycott only extends to
public men and meu of business promi
nence. John Sherman was one of the
first to go to the hotel after the declar
ation of war, and th«* morning after his
arrival he found among his letters a cir
cular telling him the hotel was in a state
of siege and that he must leave it at
once if he wished to save his social and
political future. The circular went on
to say that the Central Labor Union
was a powerful organization, and could
ruin any man socially, commercially or
politically if it so desired. Mr. Sher
man simply turned the circular over to
one of the clerks of the hotel, saying
_ t •• . . .
iiv uau uu uac ii,, rie uommuea to
stay at the hotel, notwithstanding the
circular, and has been placed on the
proscribed list. Secretary Endicott’s
experience was a similar one. Warner
Miller received his warning circular ten
days ago, but he returned again to the
Fifth Avenue, and increased his offense
by taking his wife with him. He said
he should pay no attention whatever to
the threats of the Union. Nearly
every mail brings a new grist of circu
lars from the boycotters. No name is
signed to the circular, it being prepared
in printed form and having the name of
the Central Labor Union at the bottom.
Ex-Governor Foster of Ohio found one
of the circulars in his mail and prompt
ly consigned it to the waste basket.
“No one but a demagogue,” he said,
“would pay any attention to such stuff.”
The only man the circular is reported
to have affected is Gen. Butler. He
received notice just as he was leaving
the hotel a short time ago. The clerk
at the hotel said that the General had
not returned there since.
The New York Herald thinks the
Grant Monument Committee ought by
this time to be convinced of the futility
of its plan. The committee organized
itself and advertised at the instance of
A. B. Cornell, for $1,000,000. They
have raised, in four months, a little
aver $100,000. Oiily a minority of the
committee have subscribed. The half
dozen principal managers who got up
the committee and put the 500 names
au the list, ask for a greater degree of
confidence in their discretion than the
public, or even a majority of their own
associates, are willing to accord. The
request for $1,000,000 to be contrib
uted, for them to spend on any kind
oi monument tney see fit, it is clear to
everybody but themselves, the people
at large pronounce unreasonable.
The New York Mail and Express
contains a strong indorsement by Cyrus
W. Field of Senator Edmunds’ bili re
garding Government control of the tele
graph, He says: “In my opinion the
time has come when the Government
should purchase the telegraph lines of
the country. A law should be passed
by Congress making all purchases, so
that every company should be bound
by the same principle. Each telegraph
company should Belect an arbitrator
and the Government choose another.
If these two disagree, they ought to
choose a third, who should be a man
thoroughly experienced and regarded as
an expert in the business. These Arbi
trators should have power to examiue
reports sod accounts, so as to arrive at
the actual net profits.”
The Sun's Washington special says:
Jver thirty members of the Senate and
House (about au equal number of each)
lave written to Heury Nichols, Chair
nan of the New York State Anti
Slonopoly League, commending the ob
jects of the meeting in favor of continu
ed silver coinage to be presided over by
Fdwards Fierrepont and addressed by
rhomas Ewing, John Thompson and
ethers, in New York. Warner of Ohio
ias telegraphed that he intends to be
present.
According to the examination of Dr.
boy, who testified at the inquest on the
bodies killed m the ladroad accident
near Augusta, Ga., not a single one of
the dead died from external injuries,
but all met death by being scalded and
from the inhalation of steam.

Chinatown.
One of the most sensational and
startling plots to assassinate some
twenty prominent citizens of San Fran
cisco was unearthed Tuesday night.
Some time ago the police obtained in
formation of the existence of an organi
zation called “The
SOCIALISTIC REVOLUTIONARY ASSOCIA
TION,”
which, it was asserted, was composed of
ultra socialistic members. A close
watch was kept on their movements,
and the police finally succeeded in ob
taining the minutes of one of their
meetings, held on November 23. From
the facts discovered it was found to be
the intention of the Association to “put
out of the way about twenty men, in
plmlinfr Wm T r^nlomon W W
Morrow, General VV. H. L. Barnes,
Mayor Bartlett, John Lord Love, Judge
Wilson, Judge Hoffman, Judge Sawyer,
Leland Stanford, Chas. Crocker, Chief
Crowley, Capt. Lees, Capt. Douglass,
Judge Tobin, Governor Stoneman, Col.
Bee, Capt. Short, Col. Stuart M. Tay
lor, M. H. De Young, Gen. Turnbull,
Claus Spreckles and Dennis Kearney.
These names were placed on a
“PROSCRIPTIVE LIST,’'
and placed in the hands of the Execu
tive Committee to carry out the orders
of the Association. The committee
were to devise the best means of accom
plishing the ends of the base plot, and
were thus engaged when their work was
brought to a sudden termination
by the discovery of the Associa
tion’s headquarters at 900 Montgomery
avenue, by the police, and the arrest of
four men found therein—Julius C.
Koosher, Henry Weismsn, Charles Mit
telstadt and Oscar Eggers. In the room
was also found a complete laboratory for
manufacturing infernal machines. The
men were taken to the city prison, where
they boldly asserted they were dyna
miters, and proposed to get rid of the
citizens named and then raze Chinatown.
The prisoners also belong to the Ger
man branch of the Anti-Coolie League.
WHAT THE LEADER SAID.
The leader of the band of dyna
miters arrested is Henry Weiaman,
a Bavarian by birth and a chemist by
profession, 2.') years old. He resided
with his family, 818 Pacific street, and
is President of the German Anti-Coolie
Club, and President of the Fifth Ward
Anti-Coolie League.
Un being questioned he said: “My
intention was to aid in enforcing what
O’Donnell told us was law against the
Chinese. The only thing that troubled
me was that Coroner O’Donnell was
always saying that the Chinese were
well supplied with arms, and they had
armories in Chinatown and were drilling
every night with Winchester rifles. I
was afraid that when the Chinese were
ordered to go they would resist aud
attack our people. I therefore resolved
to experiment, and see if I could not
invents superior weapon. I commenced
experiments twelve days ago and pur
chased some dynamite. My idea was
to make something that could be thrown
like a hand-grenade. We experimented
at different times under rocks near the
Cliff House, but the results were un
satisfactory. O’Douoell knew we were
experimenting. He had often talked
of dynamite and he called his newspa
per “The Anti-Coolie Dynamiter.’ ”
The bombs which were found are
made of gas-pipe from live to seven
inches long and about an inch in
diameter.”

THE WEST INDIAN IN LONDON.
A highly. iofluential ‘deputation from the
West Ladia Qomumittee waited upon the Seore
tary,of State for India on the 11'h. No de
‘tatlg of tho interview are publighed, but it has
bagn aseértained, by inquiries at the offico of
the Opmmittee, that the result was satisfactory.
No other fssue could indeed have been anti
oipated. It‘is not known what facts or argu
ments were laid before the Marquis of Salis.
bury by the deputation, or what was the exaot
abject of.the interview. But it may be said
that the lodian Government have every reason
for encouraging emigration. The density of
the population in India, now estimated at no
less than 300 miilions, the constant and inoreas
ing struggle for the bare necessarics of life,
the ever present risk of famine, the eminent
bonefits: to the coolie who takes advantage of
the means of emigration—thess are surecly
powerfal reasons. The benefits of emigration
to the West India Colonies, too, would also
weigh with a Government like that at present
in power, for the most important and glowing
passages of Mr. Disracli’s speceh at the
Lord Mayor’s dinner were devoted to the
gue-tion of serving the intercsts of the
QOolonial Empire of G:eat B.itain, and maio
taining the bond of union intact According
to appearances there never wasa time wheo
a more geoeral agreement cxisted among
people whose opinions carry weight as to thoe
advantages of coolie emigration. Even Sir
George Campbell, in a recent address to a
Scotch QOhamber “of Uommerce, did not strike
a discordant note. He apparently found it
necessary to put foiward a defence 'of his
restrictive policy in this matter during the
earlier part of his official eareer in Bengal. Tt
is easy to understand such a policy being
adopted under & misapprehension of the real
state of the case, and to do Sir G. Campbell
jostice, that, policy was not persevered in
after it had been conclusively shown that
the prejudices against the emigration were
without « shadow of foundation. There are
many points in which the working cf tho
emigration might be improved, and these have
been discussed from time to time in this col
umao. All that is wanted is that tho cystem
should be placed upon such a footing as to
develop its capabilitics to the full, and yield
the utm st benefit possible to lundia, to the
emigrating coolie, and to the cclony bhe
makes his home.

A letter has been received from a correspon
dent in Antigua relating to some comments
which were made in this column upon & memo
rial signed in that colony at the time of Gover
nor Irving’s departure, That a proper view
of the memdrial was then taken is proved by
the fact that the Secretary of State has adopt
ed the extreme course of returning it tothe
memorialists. 'L'he lctter above referred to,
and the numerous papers accompanying it,
have been read, and their conteats noted; but
as no injastice has been done in the EuroPEaN
MaIIL to the writer of the lettcr, and as at the
time the remarks he complains of were made
his name was perfectly uoknown in this coun
try in connection with the memorial, there is
no necessity to dweli further upon the matter.
The correspondent does not give a cheering
account of the present state of Antigua, and
he may rest assured that any effort he may
mako for the improvement ot the colony will
not be unappreciated at home.,

JUDICIOUS MANAGEMENT OF STA

( From the Herald.) ‘
When stable manure is exposed to the
influences of sunshine, rain, snow and dry
ing winds, a large portion of the volatile
elements will be removed so that they
will be the same as lost to the pro
prietor. Hence, all surface water shouldbe
turned away from the baroyard. Every
outbufldtng snould be- provided with-eave
troughs to cateh the water that would
find its way into the manure heap. To
prevent loss by leaching it is well to throw
over an exposed heap of excrement a cov
ering of straw or old hay. If the form
is conical nearly all the' water will run
off the sides and but little’ percolate
through. It is miserable slack and waste
ful to haul out manures into:fields: in
the autumn or winter and allow them
to remain without any protection. A
good farmer writes that durin%-the past

SHOE WORKERS' STRIKE ENDED Industry Fully Resumed in Forty Eight Factories of Lynn, Massachusetts. CONCESSIONS ON BOTH SIDES Demand for Wage Increase Is Waived, nd Court Proceedings Taken Against the Strikers Are to Be Dropped. The strike of workers affiliated with the Amalgamated Shoe Workers of America ended April 24, and work was resumed in full in the 48 factories affil iated with the Lynn Shoe Manufac turers’ association. After a conference arranged at the instance of Judge Ralph W. Reeve of the district court, officials of the Man ufacturers’ Association of the Amalga mated reached an agreement on all points at issue. The association agreed to recognize the Amalgamated, which in turn con sented to come under the terms of the agreement which the association has in operation with the United Shoe Workers of America, providing for no strikes or lockouts before April 30, 1924. The unions of Goodyear operators, heel workers and edgemakers, which started the strike on April 14, agreed to waive their demand for a 30 per cent Wage advance. The women stitchers, who Joined the strike later, agreed tok return to work, and the manufacturers promised to withdraw court proceedings in which they sought injunctions against the strik ers. The strikers were assured of re instatement without discrimination. About 5,000 workers had been on strike and an equal number had been thrown out of employment es a result. GENERAL LABOR NEWS Notices were posted at the plants of the Nashua Gummed & Coated Papei company, Nashua, N. H., announcing a wage increase of 10 per cent, effec tive April 28. Six hundred, hands are emnloved. Three more Chicago corporations have announced wage Increases. The International Paper company an nounved an advance of 8 per cent for its 7,500 employees. The Kirby Lum ber company advanced wages 15 per cent for 4,200 employees who work on dally wage basis. The Union Bag and Paper company is negotiating a wage Increase with its skilled employ ees, after increasing wages of semi skilled and unskilled workers on April 1. Preliminary figures on the employ ment situation during March in 5,453 representative establishments of 48 manufacturing industries show an In crease over the preceding month of 2.1 per cent in the number of em ployees, a 5 per cent Increase in total wages paid, and a 2.8 per cent In crease in the average weekly wage, according to a report made public by the bureau of labor statistics of the Department of Labor. Fenbeig, manager of the Auditorium theatre, booked many big attractions which will appear at his popular play house next season among them being “Blosom Time,’’ “Little Nelly Kelley,” “Abie’s Irish Rose,” “Silllie, Irene and Marv,” “The Merry Widow” and “The Fool?’ Liberty Bus Wrecked About eight o’clock Sunday night one of the big Liberty auto buses running between Columbus and Zanesville was ditched near the T. & O. C. crossing just west of Gran ville. It is claimed the accident was caused by the breaking of a front spring. The car was well filled but the passengers escaped injury. Death of Mrs. Dell Mrs. Jessie Dell, aged fifty-seven years, wife of Frank Dell, of Pine street, died at the State Hospital, Columbus, Monday morning, from a complication of diseases. In addition to her husband she is survived by four children, Miss Virginia of the home, Miss Theora of Columbus, Mrs. Bessie Stump of Columbus, and Harry Dell of Chicago one sister and one brother. Death of Charles W. Fuller Charles W. Fuller, retired drug gist, aged fifty-seven yaers, died at his home, 68 West Church street, early Monday morning, from compli cations, after an illness of several years duration. He is survived by his wife and three daughters, one sister and one brother. Funeral services were held in Trinity church Wednes day afternoon and burial made in Cedar Hill cemetery. They Got Jack Dead to Rights Jack Wooles and his side partner Ananias Andrews were caught with the goods when the room over the former’s pool room, 320 East Main street, was raided Saturday night and a party of guests caught engaged in the fascinating game of draw poker. At the hearing in municipal court the following morning Wooles pleaded guilty and was fined $25 and costs, and Andrews’ case was continued un til a later date. Saur’s Coffees have theQuality and Flavor try them. It pays Connecticut Judge favors Coolie Labor Hartford, Conn.—Leonard J. Nick erson, former speaker of the Connec ticut house of representatives, and recently appointed judge of the su perior court, favors the importation of coolie labor under five-year con tracts. “The coolie is the best laborer on earth,” said the new judge, who thus indicates his attitude toward labor’s efforts to improve conditions. “The coolie will work hard all day long, and he won’t stop at 4 o’clock, daylight time, or any other Kind of time. “But, they say. the Chinese won’t assimilate. *We don’t want them to. Send them back when their contract is up. Bring them here for a limited period, say for five years, and then send them back. “What we want is labor.” Eastern Railroad Gives Small Increase (By Federated Press) Reading, Pa.—Increase ol 4 cents an hour for all Philadelphia & Read ing mechanics and 3 cents an hour for all mechanical helpers in the mo tive power and rolling equipment de partment is effective since May 1. It was negotiated by the American Fed eration of Railroad Workers writh F. M. Falck, general manager of the road, pending a decision of the U. S. railroad labor board. Time and one half in the mechanical department for overtime in excess of eight hours and work on Sundays and holidavs is re tained and piece work remains barred. New Haven Engines Are Declared Unfit Newport, R. I.—Traffic on the New York, New Haven & Hartford rail road was held up several hours when government inspectors ordered six locomotives out of commission be cause of their dangerous condition. Engines standing on sidetracks waiting for trains were condemned, as were engines that pulled passenger trains into this city. Because .of the long delays angry passengers were in a mood to listen to the story of railroad shop men, who placed the blame for present condi tions on the anti-union policy of the New Haven management. Jones, 69 West Church street, were married at the home of the latter’s daughter, Mrs. Warner DeVoe, of Philadelphia, last Saturday afternoon. Arms loaded down with colorful straw hats of many styles, a visitor to Nassau asks direc
tions from a local traffic policeman. Beach hats double as souvenirs for tourist trade.
Head Start on Summer
FOR NEARLY three centuries, the natives
of the Bahama Islands picked hat
fibers from the swaying palms and wove
them into more or less standard styles of
sun protectors. But then came the tourist
boom. And the race was on to catch the
eye of short-time visitors with beach
bonnets that doubled as souvenirs.
As fast as Manhattan’s garment district
can copy an original Paris gown, natives
around Nassau latch onto style trends that
seem to be going well. This season in the
Caribbean sun spas the cornucopia beach
hat is out in front in the style derby. A
long, tapering cone, adorned with any kind
of tassel or trinket that looks cute, is
fashionable garb for sunbathing or in
formal cocktailing.
The crazy aspects of the new beach
wear are appreciated by the natives, who
hawk their wares in exactly that manner—
“ Buy a crazy hat, boss?” Since a cruise
ship that docks in Nassau with 500 passen
gers is likely to pull away with some 5,000
various straw items among the passengers’
luggage, the hat business has become big
business. As a straw in the trade winds,
crazy hat styles are certain to reach
Northern United States beaches this
summer

If there ever is any trouble In the
congregation about the music, and if

is admitted at once that the congre
gation and the minister are alone to
blame, writes Ian Maclaren in Ladies’
Home Journal. But there are other
difficulties, and they may be men
tioned in a spirit of becoming humil
ity. For one thing, the organist is an
artist, and every artist has a nature
of special refinement which cannot
bear the* rough-and-tumble ordinary
methods of life. With a man of
common day von deal in a practical,
straight-forward and even brutal
fashion, arguing with him, complain
ing to him, and putting him right
>\hen he is wrong. But no man must
handle precious porcelain in such fash
ion, or 4fie artist will be instantly
wounded and will resign and carry his
pathetic story to every quarter, for,
iis ,a. rul'e, the organist thinks that he
is lifted above criticism and public
opinion. It is impossible to teach
him anything: it ir an insult, to sup
pose that anything could be better
than the music he provides.

atische Lloyd about Protestant lady
rnissioners in the far east are worth

more the fashion for European and
American ladies to fulfill their mis
sionary ^vocation by marrying China
men. Scarce half a year ago a Swedish
lady missionary set the unfortunate
example of marrying a Chinese coolie,
thus seriouslyjnjuring her missionary .
society, and, in general, compromis
ing the respect in which missionaries
are held. There is now a case in Can
ton of a lady belonging to an Amer
ican mission. Miss Halverson, marrv
ng her former cook. There is a great
sensation about it in missionary cir
cles, and it is really difficult to under
stand how a lady, wdio goes to China
n order to help Christianize, can, after
so short a time, do more harm to it
lhan long years of labor will make
jood.”—JNT. Y* Sun.

miles were carefully charted in differ
ent parts of the world—Asia, Africa
ami the South Pacific.

text:
GROWTH AND '
WORK OF M. M.L
WHAT COLONEL MI'HKEE IS DOING
AT marion.
Able FanltT ml Modern Ideas of Edu
cation Make Military Iaatltate
Prominent Parlor In Ala
bama—Ita Illatory.
BY VICTOR H. HANSON.
Marlon. Aug. 5.—(Staff Correspond
ence.)—Those who have followed the
progress of education in the South
have observed with profound Interest
the foundation and growth of the Ma
rlon Military Institute. Elsewhere in
this Issue Is an interesting sketch of
the school.
From this it appears that no one but
a professional educator of known abil
ity and successful experience shall be
eligible to the office of chief executive.
The signal success which has attend
ed this Institution and the auspicious
era upon which it Is now entering, is
due to this wise provision of the found
ers.
The superintendent of the Institute.
Colonel James Thomas Murfee. was
admirably qualified to undertake the
great work of founding and directing
an educational Institution of the high
est order. He was graduated with first
honors from the Virginia Military In
stitute in 18SS. Immediately after
graduation he undertook the work to
which he has devoted fifty-two years
of his singularly successful life. After
his early labors in Madison College
and In Lynchburg College, he came to
the State of Alabama In 1860. He was
Professor of Mathematics and Com
mandant of Cadets at the University of
Alabama for five years. For seventeen
years ho was President of Howard
College at Marlon. On the removal of
(Howard College to East Lake in 1887,
(be remained at Marlon to build the Ma
rlon Military Institute.
Rich In experience and wisdom from
A long and successful life as an edu
cator, he Is now assisted in the work
ffcy his two sons, both distinguished
imasters of arts of the University of
Virginia. One has served as fellow in
(Mtrooomy at the University of Vir
Inla, as Instructor in mathematics at
he University of Virginia and as fel
Row, lecturer and research assistant In
hyslos at the University of Chicago,
other has taken the Bachelor of
and Master of Arts of the Unl
■Itjr of Virginia, has pursued grnd
te study at the University of Chl
__go, and has visited the famous Eng
lish Sphools at Rugby, Eton, Wlnches
fier and Harrow.
• The Institute Is singularly fortunate
possessing the services of two such
Betlngntsfaed scholars to carry on the
h*Dt4c so ably begun by Its honored su
■pertntendent and founder. The high
{recognition which has been accorded
krbe work of the Institute by the great
AMitversUIes and the eminent educators
of America, Is a worthy tribute to the
wisdom and ability of those who have
Administered its affairs.
HONG KONG AT NIGHT.
Scone Queer Street Scene* of a Queer
City.
Correspondence In London Pall Mall
Garotte.
A moment ago the peak was blanket
ad with a dense fog. It was as If the
ebon wings of death hovered over the
Island. But now the Chinese mainland
feve&ls Itself and beyond the harbor
and beyond Kowloon, and beyond the
jpaddy fields, the great mountains stand
out black and wonderful against the
•ky. The moon is racing Between the
eloud gaps and burnishing the face of
the waters and gleaming on the wetted
Sides of the steamers. Far to south
you can Just see the light house, the
one-eyed sentry of Gap Rock, blinking
e—the masthead lights of the ships that
puss in the night. Between the light
and Hongkong is the China Sea studded
With islands, and in and out among the
Islands great Junks with bat-wing sails
move like phantom things over the
leaden sea.
Far below the peak is the harbor, lit
up by the liners and the cruisers and
the Junks and the sampans that lie as
thick as ants on a honey-pot. It Is
the harbor of a million eyes, and a
coolie leaning against a rickshaw lik
ens it unto Heaven, top-side up. De
scending the mountain side-brings you
to the harbor. Here is a little knot of
people on the quay, and in front of
them a chow squatting on his haunch
es with a bamboo sticking out over the
water. From the end of the bamboo
four strings run down and out, pyramid
fashion, into the water. The bamboo
trends, and the putty-faced man on the
Quay rises and lifts the bamboo over
Als head. The four strings become
ktaut. and out of the black water a net
appears swarming with small fry, and
.the putty-faced man is pleased. I flsh
,ed all day over the side of a tramp and
caught nothing. The Chinese firemen,
coming out of the blackness of the
irtokehold, their bare backs streaming
mrlth Ink, looked at me with utter con
tempt. Yet here is this grinning coolie
sinking his ragged net anywhere In the
harbor and performing miracles. His
ancestors must have dwelt on the shores
of Galilee.
The sampons and the Junks have all
anchored off some distance from the
quay. This is the law of Hong Kong,
for otherwise the army of sampan men
knight step ashore and seize the island
In the dark watches of the night. The
qjuav Is full of life, for the night is
warm and the merchants, arrayed in
gmrple and fine linen, are strolling in
the moonlight. Mr. Wokes is attired In
a flowing robe of white and pale blue
baggy trousers. Trotting beside him is
little Wokee Junior, arrayed in splen
dor gaudy as a butterfly. The children
are racing up and down, their rat-tailed
queues tied with bows of pink and blue.
Over the water from the lined-up sam
pans comes a woman’s voice. She is
droning a Cantonese love song. There
Is 6 feet 2 of Sikh police In khaki uni
form. His beard is shining in the lamp
light; it la well trimmed and black as
Jet. Around his left wrist are two half
hfttches of pigtail, and on the end of
the pigtail is a coolie revolving and
wriggling like a kangaroo rat in a
trap. The coolie is a desperate fellow;
you can see that by his face, a face that
must have broken all the command
--ments of the East and West. There is
c huge crowd following. It looks as
(If every coolie In Hong Kong had form
led up in the scrum behind the Sikh.
'Stray pieces of road metal strike him,

A Select School for Girls and Young Ladies.
Motto: “That our daughters may be corner stones polished after the similitude of a palace.”—Ps. 1/4-12.
R. J. HOLSTON, A M., President.
Tuscaloosa Female College.
and Conservatory of Music.
R. J. Holston, A. M., and Rev. J. M. Qreene, Assocfate
Presidents, Queen City Avenue, Tuscaloosa, Ala.
Beautiful Illustrated Purple and Gold Catalogue Free!
Next Session Opens Sept. 14th.
The Ideal school for the development of well-rounded womanhood. Ex
cellent moral Influences, careful Instruction by specialists, unexcelled
health record, attractive home life, large, well-shaded campus, good music
hall furnished with beautiful pianos, well equipped gymnasium, special
preparation for Sophomore and Junior classes In University of Alabama, In
which our graduates take specially hi gh rank. Expenses quite moderate.
Write for beautiful catalogue. ■
HISTORY.
The Tuscaloosa Female College elates
back as a school to the year 1836, when
Its doors were first thrown open as the
house of the teacher and the taught.
This entitles It the oldest Female Col
lege in Alabama, and one of the oldest
In the t’nlted States. It opened the
first day with five pupils, but grew
very rapidly under the management of
the Rev. John I)agg. It was then
known as the Athenetim. It had quite
a successful career for a number of
years, then passed through various
hands, and vicissitudes, and in 1852 i
number of gentlemen purchased th<
property and organized It into a Meth
odist school with Mr. Russell as presi
dent. In the fall of 1856 Rev. J. M
Bonnell took charge and continued hi:
labors here till the middle of the ses
slon *59-60. Bishop R. II. Hargrovi
was at that time on the Board of Trus
tees. On the 2nd day of August, 1858
It was chartered under the name o
Tuscaloosa Female College, with L. C
SECTION OF LIBRARY.
Garland, E. B. Vaughan and F. F.
Hemphill as trustees. It Is very Inter-1
estlug history to state that two of
the daughters of Mr. Hemphill now
teach In this Institution.
In 1876 the school had declined, as
all schools do In time, then appeared
Mr. Alonzo Hill, a man with strong
mind and fine executive ability, who
purchased the property and by his un
tiring energy and perseverance raised
it to rank among the first Institutions
of our Southland. For seventeen and
a half years he gave it his undivided
attention. Suddenly In the middle of
the session Mr. Hill was tnken away,
and the next year the property was
purchased by the North Alabama Con
ference and Rev. J. 1). Simpson was
made President. Dr. Simpson held the
position for two or three years, and
was beloved by every one. When he
retired, Dr. W. F. Melton was made
President, and ran the College suc
cessfully for six years. In June, 1903,
it fell Into the hands of the present
administration, whose sole aim Is to
make It and maintain It as one of the
leading Institutions of the State.
The school has enjoyed a prosperous'
career. It would be interesting to take
records of various years nnd see the
number of pupils that have had their
minds and hearts fashioned within
these walls. The institution may point
with pride to Its graduates, many of
whom adorn society, and make homes
happy by their Intelligence and virtue,
anil many of whom have taken a stand
slve additions and improvements in
the last few years, and will continue to
be Improved and added to from year to
year, which largely increases Its capa
city, and adds greatly to its archi
tectural beauty. Connected with this
by halls are the school buildings, con
sisting of College, Preparatory, Art,
Laboratory, Recitation, Sewing, Gym
nasium and Music Rooms. These are
ail well ventilated and easily warmed,
and are supplied with the necessary
furniture and apparatus. The private
rooms of the pupils are comfortably
furnished.
The city has a splendid system of
waterworks, and the College buildings
are supplied throughout with a abund
ance of water. The College has bath
rooms. with hot and cold water, electric
lights and bells, telephones and other
conveniences.
('ouNprvolory of Music.
The ensuing year will be marked by
one of the most significant strides made
in the Department of Music.
Home Department.
The President and his family reside
in the College buildings, as do the
teachers. There are three or four
teachers on every floor, so that pupils
come in contact with teachers, not only
while in the schoolroom, but all the
time. This brings them under the very
best influence and makes everything
homelike.
All the pupils are required to attend
the regular Chapel services every morn
-^
in the foremost ranks of the great
army of teachers.
Location Ideal.
Tuscaloosa is picturesquely situated
in the midst of hills which obstruct the
course of the Black Warrior Ktver.
The intersection of three important
railroads, the A. G. S.. M. & O. and W.
S., with sixteen daily passenger trains
makes tills city easily accessible to all
sections of the country. We arc fifty
six miles west of Birmingham; ninety
seven miles east of Meridian; sixty
miles south of Columbus, and one hun
dred and seven miles north of Mont
gomery
This Is a beautiful town, and its lo
cation largely exempts it from ma
larial diseases and epidemics.
Its winters are mild and genial,
while the h* at of summer is tempered
by the shade of the giant oaks which
abound on every street.
Its churches, representing the dif
ferent religious denominations, are
large and beautiful, and served by
ministers noted for their piety, learn
ing and eloquence.
It is the seat of the University of
Alabama, which, together with the
Colleges and academies, has made the
town noted for its educational advan
tages. This is a city 01 morality and
refinement, a most desirable location
for an institution of learning.
Building*, Furniture, Etc.
The main college building is a com
modious. convenient and handsome
brick edifice. It has received exten
Ing. On Sunday they attend the church
nnd Sabbath-school of their parents’s
choice. The school Is entirely non
sectarian. but strictly Christian. A
high record for personal religion, and
reverence for the Word of God, are
Inculcated on all suitablo ocaslons. The
President and teachers cherish a con
stant. and prayerful solicitude that theli
pupils should prize the "pearl of great
price" above all earthly goods. It If
their aim to Inculcate within the pupil
sincere, cheerful piety, as the foun
dation of good character, the well
spring of abiding happiness.
The Bible.
The Bonk of books fs not neglected
It Is studied 111 the regular course, am
It Is astonishing with how much in
terest girls study the Bible.
Music.
As has already been mentioned thl:
department has received *.hc most care
ful attention. The very best talen
this country -nffords has ben secured.
We have a splendid music hall, wel
equipped with all new Instruments, ant
a new concert grand.
Gymnastic*.
We have connected with the Ctdlcgi
a well-equipped Gymnasium Hall. At
advantage but few Southern Colleges
have. This Is very beneficial to girls
In Physical Culture, and they love It.
Art.' Kloeutlon and Physical Culture
will be under the rare of a most skill
ed instructor, Miss Sullivan, of Virginia
She is a full graduate of Montgomery
Female College. She took post-grad
uate work at Stonewall Jackson Insti
tute, Abingdon, Vs. She took Studio
work from the noted artist, Bashfleld.
She studied at Cooper Institute, New
York, and has had a most prosperous
oarer with a number of years experi
ence.
Miss Sullivan teachers the Delsarte
Method In Elocution, and while In New
York her Instructors were Professor
Mann and Miss Vail. The art of Ex
pression is becoming very popular, and
Is one of the most Interesting features
of our College.
Industrial and Practical Branches.
These have not been neglected, and
have been fairly well represented In our
institution.
Our course of study Is as extensive
as any Female College of the State, and
much higher than most of them.
Physical Culture.
We place health above everything
else, because a sound body is essential
to sound, active minds, and is a potent
and almost Indispensable factor In
pleasant and successful living.
Knowing how much pupils need these
exercises all boarding pupils are re,
quired to take physical culture about
twice a week under direction of a
trained teacher. For tills a small fef
of $5.00 per term will be charged. This
Is required of till boarding pupils and
must be paid at beginning of term. Day
pupils may also take by paying th«
fee
Pupils are advised to take special ex
erclacs in gymnastic work. They wll
And It of great Importance In the pre
servation of their health and strengtl
while In College work.
Special Advantages.
This Institution relies for patronagi
solely upon the superior eharaoter o;
the advantages offered. As it is unen
dowed, and must be sustained by thi
annual income, the public may be as
sured that no effort nor reasonable ex
pense will be spared to keep every de
partment up to th€ highest state of
proficiency.
The patronage of the school comes
from the most refined and cultured
families of the Stale; hence the influ
ences and associations are of the very
best character. Our capacity for board
ing pupils is limited to eighty-five, and
we have as many teachers boarding
in the College as many schools have to
accommodate three or four hundred
boarders. It is obvious, therefore, that
our girls get more and better attention
than is usual in boarding schools* Wa
do not hesitate to claim for this and oil
frlvate schools an element of personal
raining which it is impossible for a
pupil to obtain in the public schools or
in State institutions where large masses
of pupils from all walks of life ars
brought together.
The Charges.
The charges are believed to bo as
fmoderate as are made by any school
for the same advantages In instruction,
board and accommodation. We are not
trying to make a cheap school. We
have secured the best teacherjC'We could
find, and, of course, we have to pay
such teachers good salaries. We could
have filled the College with teachers
whose salaries would have cost us less
than half what these we have employed
will cost us. Hut an honest business
man wants the best for his money and
would rather pay a little r»ore in order
to get the best.
Summer School.
There is a summer school held at ths
University of Alabama every year for
the bentflt of those expecting to teach
This opens soon after our session closes
and continues six weeks.
Young ladies who want to take ad
vantage of this will find It convenient
to attend the Tuscaloosa Female Col
leg© and contlnue£-here at the summer
school We are only}a few blocks from
I the University, and have agreed to
board a number ot young ladles during
the summer sessions.
but he is not afraid, and his eyes are
flashing and grand to behold.
The crowd is pressing in closer and
showing its long yellow teeth. Their
wiry hands, armed with Inch linger
nails, stab at the Sikh. Front his
right wrist dangles a baton. The
baton gives an angry little jerk now
and then like a dog's leg twitching
with pain. A coolie, more desperate
than the rest, conies within range, but
the Sikh has eyes in the hack of his
head. The long, loose arm with the
baton at the end swings round like a
catapult, and there is a nasty, dull
thud. The coolie sits in the dust rub
bing tobacco into a two-inch scalp
wound and wondering what lie has
been doing since morning. Along the
quay six more Silths reinforce their
comrade, and the crowd melts away to
the gambling dens nild the dice box.
Queen Street, the fashionable quar
ter of Hong Kong, Is almost deserted,
and Is cheerless, for there are no
lights in the windows. There must
lje a dog light In tlio Chinese quarter,
and all the Queen Street folk have
gone there. You go that way. too. and
as you draw near a sound of revelry
by night falls on your ears. A steam
er's siren hoots down on the quay.
There is a thish of brass and tinkling
of cymbal*.' and a volley of crackers
from the steamer's bows. If that pan
demonium otherwise knowtn as Chinese
music has not terrified the "debll man"
It Is not the Chinaman's fault. I hear
tkx 'h.id uf the river steamer's paddles
and the shouts and laughter of the
coolie passengers. The Sum-Stn Is off
to Canton.
The glow of light from the Chinese
quarter almost blinds you at llrst. The
streets are full of lanterns, red and yel
low and green and blue, and blue and
green and yellow and red are some of
the colors that adorn the crowd. 1
hear the sound of the scraping of fid
dles over the way. and step over and
look In. At one end of the room are
about twenty sailors, British and
American, sitting on a long bench. At
the other a bar with an array of beer
mugs. It looks like target practice
Behind the bar Is a woman, the one
white woman I have seen tonight, and
her reminiscences will run to thirty
editions. In the corner Is a low plat
form about one foot high and five feet
square, and on this two Filipinos
scraping violins. The sailors set to
partners, these that can stand, and
circle slowly around the room with a
kind of movement that has the rock
of the sea In It. The sailors who can
not stand sit where they are and ap
plaud. Everything seems a great suc
cess to them. Tomorrow they will he
cooling their heads on the capstan.
A girl of 18 Is feeling her way along
the shop fronts, her face painted as
gaudy as a barber's pole. Her dress is
a dress of a million dyes, and all the
shades of sunset are blended there. Out
of her mouth, expressionless, putty
face two beady eyes peep between the
slanting lids. She must b« blind. But
)
no! She is groping along’ the shop
fronts because h»re midget shoes arc
only two nnd a half inches long. And
if you stood her out in the road she
would tumble over like a wooden doll.
Little Chinese policemen with mush
room hats patrol the winding streets.
They surely cannot keep order. A half
an hour ago a coolie thought likewise,
hut now he is quite of another opinion
as he sits In a cell nursing a lump on
his sculp as big as a saucer.
Here is a restaurant. It has a front
like a Buddlst temple. Some local
swell has .lust entered. Bang! bang! |
bang! go the cymbal men Inside the
door. That Is In his honor.
A turn in the street and you see the
harbor and the harbor lights. There are
half a dozen black shadows rising out
of the water, the armour-plate sides of
cruisers. Within those walls are men
of Devon. Another long. low. black al
ligator thing with two stumpy funnels
is moving swiftly out of the harbor
like a thief in the night. It is a de
stroyer off on p atrol.
I have not seen a drunken man as
yet, and this is the poorest quaFter of
the town. What a contrast to the al
leys of Dnmboth and the faces of the
wild beasts that scavenge the dust bins
there!
The shutters ere closing, the streets
are emptying and I notice only one
Sikh policeman and two mcn-o’-wars
men steering their sinuous way to the
quay side. How beautiful and wonder
ful is lbs sXt! It 1* (tnieCw still. I
pass a shop and through a crack In the
shutters streams a faint light| I pass
through and into the room beyond the
shop and notice a dozen putty-faced
men bending over a low table. I hear
the rattle, the rattle of an inverted dice
cup. It is the first sound a Chinaman
hears at birth. He will listen for it
when he wakes on Judgment Day.
THE “NIAGARA. MOVEMENT.”
Southern Negroes Warned Agnlnst the
Plan of Certain Radical Agitator*.
Charlotte (N. C.) Observer.
Poor Booker Washington! He <s as
sailed from within and without. Wheth
er he ride in the Pullman parlor car or
the Jim Crow, lie catches it "er cornin'
an* ov gwine." But with Mr. Carnegie's
SUOO.AOO given him that he may not wor
ry and fret over the trivial things of
life, perhaps Booker will be able to
stand it.
There is a row in the negro camp,
though. There always has been, it
seems, and still “there is razors er Ilyin*
in do air." Literally was this true a
ft w years ago when Dr. Booker Wash
ington spoke, or attempted to speak, in
Boston. The Boston negroes don't like
Booker. He is too much of a sure
enough “nigger” for them. They want
to be white, want to learn Latin and
Greek, instead of how to plow and
pick colt on; prefer philosophizing to
pulling fodder, working their jaws in
stead of thoir arms and legs. and. above
i all. object to tlxe "Jim Crow” and the
black skin. This class of negroes is
led by on" W. E. B. EuBois of Atlanta,
who is the author of a most beautifully
written and a most viciously conceived
book called 'The Souls of Black Folks."
In mourning for the things that are not.
as a piteous wall, nerve-racking and
soul-torturing, the Lamentations of
Jeremiah are hilarious lo-sido it. Du
Uois is a New England negro. So far
as ids skin Is concerned, l-o could pass
anvwhere for an Anglo-Saxon, as he is
practically, having, however, the one
drop of blood In him that makes him a
negro lie is perhaps the ties' educated
and the most scholarly negro In Anur
lea. and one of the greatest disturbing
elements In tact relationships. Born
and educated in New England. In the
New England atmosphere, with New
England ideals and with New England
money, he has been. ev. r since he came
to maturity, a sower of discontent and
discord. For years be has been raising
a constant clamor against all the efforts
cf both races toward harmony of spirit
and co-operation in the upward strug
gle. Now he has originated what is
palled the “Niagara Movemenl." a con
ference of leading negroes from all
parts of the country. Inaugurated last
week at Niagara Falls The chief of
Ject and tenet of this "movement" seems
to be opposition to Booker Washing
ton's gospel of work and material prog
ress- letting political and social ad
vancement severely alone. It Is com
posed of the same school of negroes
that need raxors on Booker Washing
ton In Boston ana the same that be
haves Insolently to white people and
supercilliously to black people every
where. Our Southern negroes would be
far better off to have none of them.
Booker Washington, whatever mistakes
he may have made. Is a far safer and
wiser leader of his race than 3uch mal
contents as DuBols.
English Iloy Climbs Wettcrhorn.
The ascent of the \V* tterhorn from
Grlndelwald by an English boy of 14
certainly constitutes a notable record.
Probably the record at the other end
of life Is held by the great Grlndciwald
guide. Christian Aimer, who celebrated
his gold* n wedding In company with
Ills family on the peak In lS'.tfi. The
Watterhorn was Aimer’s own mountain.
He was said..never to be happy If he
did not ascend it at least once In a
season every year since he took part
as a lad in Justice Willis's first ascent
of the mountain In 1854. and planted a
young fir tree on the summit. Nowa
days the Wetterhorn is lightly refer
red to as a ' ladies' mountain." but two
terrible disasters In one week, of Au
gust. 1902. showed that It Is to be ros
Another Victim.
"Poor Mrs. Boozer suiters terribly
from the liquor habit," said Mrs. Gabb.
"How is that?” Inquired Mrs. Oiling
scenting gossip.
"When her husband comes home at
night he Is toff far gone ,o pay atten
tion to her remonstrances, and the next

[can’t listen to her." ^

THE EIGHT STAGES*,
^ Orly a baby,
f Kissed and caressed,
Gently held to a mother's breast*
^ Only a child.
Toddling alone,
Brightening now Its happy homo, ^
Only a boy,
Trudging to school, *
Governed now by a sterner rule.
Only a youth,
Living in dreams.
Full of promise life now 6eema.
Only a man.
Battling with life,
Shared in now by a loving wife.
Only a father.
Burdened with care.
Silver threads In dark brown hair.
Only a gray beard.
Toddling again.
Growing old and full of pain.
Only a mound,
0'ergrown with gross.
Dreams unrealized—rest at last.
—Boston Traveller
I MY STRANGE PASSENGER.
I -
I We were on our way from Hong Kong to
Foochow on the coasting steamer Namoa,
writes Ernest TTilkinson, U. S. N., in The
.Washington Evening Stas, when Capt. N.,
my "fidus Achates” of the voyage, looted at
the gathering clouds to the westward and re
marked: “I hope it won’t rain before we get
into Amoy; we are just thirty miles away.”
“How do you know the distance so exactly,
captain?”
“Look at that rock, and over tatyond it you
can see through a rift in the clouds a little
speck like a pin point on the top of that black
mountain. The pin point is a tall pagoda on
that high cliff,>aud the pagoda is as good a sign
board for this town as if that whole black
cliff were painted in white letters a mile high
and half a mile wide—A-H-O- Y. I never see
either the pagoda or the city that a cold chill
does not run over me.”
“Will you tell me why, captain?”
“Certainly, but it’sa long story; well, here
goes:
Amoy was one of the first treaty ports in
China open to foreign commerce, and for a
long time the noted hatred of the Chinese for
foreigners was more intense than at any other
port. I was then captain of a steamer on the
eline plying between Amoy and the Eng
colony of Hong Kong, some two hundred
miles away.
One cloudy evening in November 1 went
ashore in Amoy to make a few final prepara
tions for my ship’s departure the next day.
While on shore I noticed that my footsteps
were dogged by a disreputable looking coolie,
who approaohed me with great earnestness
in his manner as soon as we had renched a
comparatively open spot, where the growing
darkness shut out the teeming hordes of a
Chinese city. I saw that it would be impos
sible to avoid an encounter if this strange
follower should prove to be a highwayman.
So carrying my hand to my hip pocket,
where I felt the friendly “grip" of my re
volver, I waited for the man to come closer.
1 then saw that the eoolie was in great dis- j
tress, and moved by his earnestness, I stopped j
to listen to bis tale. The man explained, in
Chinese and “pidgin” English, that ho had a
brother in jail who was to be beheaded in
two days more for capsizing in a sailboat and
drowning his passenger, a mandarin’s son.
The mandarin, bent on revenge, had thrown
the boatman into prison, where the farce of
a trial had been gone through with, and the
innocent man had been doomed to die. The
coolie said that his family were all wretchedly
poor, but that they had managed, by the sale
of most of their belongings, to raise money
enough to bribe the jailer to allow the prisoner
to escape, and all that was necessary to save
his life was to get him away on some vessel to
the nearest foreign colony. The. poor creat
ure fell on his knees and implored me to save
,his brother’s life.
¥ Hs would give me anything—everything
henad—only to give the hunted creature a
hiding place, to save a fellow being from the
headsman’s axt All this was muttered be
tween broken sobs, and the poor man wept
as if it were he himself who was only to see
two more suns rise before the earth would
drink np his life blood.
X mt uij Bj’Uipatuico uinvuocij vav.>«cu(
and yet I knew the treacherous nature of the
Chinese and the danger in interfering with
their Maas of justice, and, wishing either to
test the truth of hts story or to prevail upon
the coolie to choose some other means for his
brother's escape, I said: “I’ll stow him away
and carry him down to Hong Kong for five
hundred taels” taboiit five hundred and fifty
dollan), thinking that such a price would be
•utterly beyond the ooohe’s means. The poor
man seemed staggered at the enormity of the
sum, a large fortune to one of his class; but
he rallied in a moment and said he supposed
he would have to pay it; that it was a fear
ifntpnm, that he was very poor, and to raise
SO much money his family would have to sell
all they owned; but he must save his broth
er’s'Bfs; if the captain insisted he would
lhaveto pay ft.
t Hy sympathies were now still more keenly
aroused, and seeing that the unwelcome pas
senger would be sent, and not oaring either
to break my word or to profit by the poor
wretch’s misfortunes, I said: “Well, I’ll do it
for the regular fare (about ten dollars); bring
Wm down to the wharf at 11:30; I am going
off to my ship then.”
The coolie seemed overpowered with joy,
and was still “kow-towing” his thanks as I
moved away and he disappeared in the dark
ness.
I had no sooner reached the wharf, about
11:45 p. m., then I was touched by the same
coolie, who now offered himself as “sampan”
man. I followed him to the boat, and there
saw another man as poorly clad as his broth
er. When we shoved off I noticed both
were very clumsy with their oars, but as my
ship was close to the wharf we were soon
alongside.
Hero I lianrlaH my overcoat to the boatman
and he picked up a bundle tied Chinese fash
ion in a large handkerchief, and we weut on
board, leaving my new acquaintance in the
boat. I sent my steward forward on an er
rand that would [detain him for a few mo
ments, and then had the coolie deposit his
bundle in a small closet in the cabin and told
him that that must be his brother’s hiding
place until we put to sea, and that he must
be quick to get into it.
At a motion over the side the condemned
man sprang out of the boat, which he had
made fast to the gangway, and slid noise
lessly aft through, the cabin and into the
closet I turned the lock and put the key in
my pocket .
But as he pawed the MKgn ■ lamp, curiosity
had led me to take a deSPhing glance at my
strange passenger, and, in spite of his un
kempt hair and soiled and tattered clothes,
his light complexion and refined features re
vealed in the coolie’s brother (?) a Chinaman
of the higher classes.
I then tried to scrutinize the boatman, but
the man’s back was to the light, and, the
steward returning just then, I paid my sam
pan fare, and my strange acquaintance de
parted.
1 turned in, wondering who my mysterious
passenger might be, and my thoughts were
not without vague misgivings of the noted
treachery of the Chinese.
I woke early, and had hardly begun dress
ing before a herald came to inform mo that
the viceroy of the province desired to see me
at his “yuroe^s” at 10 that morning. This
strange summons I at once connected with
my harboring an escaped prisoner, and, full
of vague distrust, I had almost decided either
to put to sea two hours before the advertised
time, noou, and so temporarily avoid any ex
planations, or to plead press of business, and
refuse to obey an almost royal command.
Disturbed by such doubts, I hardly felt re
lieved when another herald came to say that
the viceroy had concluded, as he desired to
see the ship, to visit the captain, and that my
presence at the “yamen” would be excused.
Such a thing as a viceroy visiting in state
a merchant vessel was almost unprecedented,
and I began to fear that I was implicated in
the escape of a political prisoner of high
I nuiK.
j Now tho customary official messengers be
| gan to pour in—first, two clad in robes of
state announced that his excellency would
arrive in half an hour; then four more that
he was coming in ten minutes; then four
horsemen, gaudily caparisoned, rode down
to the wharf, where 1 was now waiting, to
say that their master would arrive in five
minutes; then a procession of liveried ser
vants, bearing aloft on high poles red sign
boards, on which all the virtues under the
sun were ascribed in Chinese characters to
their lord; soldiers with flags and swords and
spears, men with whips and gongs to clear
the way; mandarins on horseback; lictors
with long pheasants’ tails in their caps, and
a large nibble on foot—all proclaimed that
his excellency had arrived. Alighting from
a gorgeous green sedan chair, borne by six
teen men in livery, he greeted me most po
litely and accepted my invitation to take
passage in my gig oft to the ship. A large
portion of his escort followed, occupying a
small fleet of sampans.
The viceroy was ushered into the cabin,
and, strange to say, selected a chair imme
diately in front of the door of the closet in
which the refugee was concealed.
After a few courtesies had been exchanged,
1 was informed through an interpreter that
Prince Ichang, the leader of an insurrection,
who had been captured and condemned to be
beheaded, had made his escape. Suspicion,
they said, seemed to point to his being se
creted on board my ship; a sampan had been
seen to go alongside of her the night before
about midnight; it reached the ship with two
boatmen and one foreigner, and returned to
the shore with only one man, and he made
off in great haste as soon as he had landed,
leaving the sampan adrift. ‘‘Of course the
captain knew nothing about the escaped pris
oner, and so he could have no objections to
allowing the ship to be searched.”
This was subtly put. To refuse to allow it
would be equivalent to acknowledging that
the man was on board, and would cost me
my place in a company whoso interest it was
to placate the unfriendly Chinese. To allow
the ship to be searched involved the possible
discovery of the man, and in that case his re
capture and certain death, as well as my own
dismissal from the company.
Either course might endanger the lives of
the foreign community in Amoy, against
whom the hatred of the Chinese needed only
a pretext to begin a general massacre. I felt
the color come and go in my cheeks, and for
a moment I thought of delivering the refugee
up to certain death, saying that when 1 took
him on board I was not aware of the nature
of the offense, and then revulsion of feeling
came over me. 1 thought “this man has
trusted his life in my hands and, hunted crim
inal that be is, I will not betray him.”
All this flashed through my mind in an in
stant, and when I turned to the viceroy I felt
the same spirit of helpless yet indomitable de
fiance that every true sailor feeis in the fury
of the storm. I said quietly: “Certainly,
your excellency; my steward will turn over
the keys to your servants, but they will find
no such man on board my shin.”
The search party went all over the ship,
directed by the crew, and after probing into
corners and peering in amongst the bales oi
silk and boxes of tea, no stranger was found.
This was reported to the viceroy, who said:
“You have not searched this cabin; do so.”
I was wild with excitement and alarm, but
my relief was intense when my furtive glance
showed me that the search party did not dare
to ask their master to move from in front of
the door. This relief was of short duration,
for he again asked if they had searched
everywhere. “Everywhere except in that
apartment behind your excellency’s chair.
We will look there, too; where is the key!”
I now became thoroughly frightened, and
fumbling for some loophole to escape,
I told the viceroy that that was a locker
where I kept my wines, and—I was ashamed
to confess it to so high aruler under the “Sou
of Heaveu"—that I sometimes hid opium and
other contraband articles there. Would his
excellency forgive me if I begged that that
place be left unopened, as my peccadilloes. If
discovered, would cost ms my post as cap
tain. “In that cast,* said the viceroy, “1
will save you from trouble by inspecting my
self; the hey?”
Doubtful whether to confess my complicltj
oi’ to brave it through, I thought of the mys
terious nature of the whole affair, and hoped
that the strange passenger might in some
mysterious manner have escaped. This straw
of hope that drowning desperation clung to
saved the day. I reached in my pocket, and
with trembling fingers pulled out thq key.
The viceroy unlocked the door, opened it,
and closed it hastily behind him.
My nerves were then so wrought upon that
I could almost have heard the dew fall, and I
fancied I heard a word within spoken very
low. Then the door opened again, and there
was a rustle of silken robes, the door closed,
and the viceroy said in Chinese, “No one
there I” v
I felt my heart throb with one great bound,
and things seemed to reel around me. When
I recovered my composure enough to look up
«1th pleased and grateful eyes I saw an ex
pression I thought I recognised, and in an in
stant I knew what my own unstrung nerves
and the regal robes had before concealed—
the miserable coolie of the night before was
none other than the viceroyof the province
of Fuh-kied, the absolute riiRr of twenty-five
million of people. I had no longer a doubt
that my mysterious passenger and the royal
fugitive were the same, and that the viceroy
himself was conniving at his escaijo.
The ship sailed on time, and Prince Icbang
was landed safely In Hong Kong, where he
lived under English protection until a severe
Alness let him have that privilege most mor
tals enjoy—of dying with his bead on.
Subsequent developments pointed to the
fact that the viceroy was influenced not only
by personal friendship, but by an enormous
bribe with which the rich prince bought his
head, and that, fearing the treachery of anj
of his subordinates, he had planned and exe
cuted the escape entirely alone. Of my bo
traying him he had-no fear, as ths word of i
“foreign devil !n would then weigh nothing ir
a Chinese court.
Two years afterward I received from tin
viceroy of Kwang Tung a gorgeous pair o!
vases and some magnificent embfoideries, “ii
gratitude for past hospitalities,” and I fount
that my coolie friend had been promoted t<
the government of ono of the largest prov
tnces of the empire,—True Flarr.
AN ENIGMA.
Kind and cruel Is her play,
Fierce and gentlo both Is she.
Fickle as the winds astray.
Constant as the heavens be;
Her caprices without number
Every shape and color take.
Sometimes Joyous, sometimes somber,
Smiles and tears together wake.
Child she is, and woman, too;
Naught more mild or harsh can be.
Whati'you think I speak of you?
Nay! I’m talking of the sea.
Now 'tia troubled, now 'tis calm;
Now It soothes like song of birds—
Often angry, then like balm,
Lisping sweet and loving words.
Devil ’tis and angel bright.
Now inviting, now repelling,
Its fair face and blue eyee light
Woo men down to death's cold dwelling;
Naught more mild or harsh can be.
Wound it gives, but healing, too;
What I you think I mean the sea?
Dearest, nay! I speak of you!
—Boston Herald.
Around the World in Sixty-nine Days.
A copy of The London Times was sent
around the world, three years ago, through
Canadian Pacific connections, in less than
sixty-nine days. This occurred without pre
arrangement, and a similar trip could be
mode today in much shorter time. The Mar
quis of Huntley, while in Japan, reoeived a
copy of The Times via Suez, on the day a
Canadian Pacific steamer was sailing for Van
couver, and it occurred to him to try the ex
periment.
He forwarded the paper by mail to The
Times with the result of its reaching the
office the sixty-ninth day following its date
of issue. This time, however, can soon be
reduced to fifty-eight days, for the fast steam
ships of the Canadian Pacific, now building,
will soon be plowing the serene waters con
necting Vancouver with Hong Kong. The
trip from Loudon to this British port in the
Orient can then be, easily accomplished in
twenty-eight days, and the average time of
the Peninsular and Oriental steamers between
Hong Kong and London, via Suez and Brin
disi, is but thirty days.—Walter P. Phillips
in Journalist.
The Angry Tree.
There is a species of acacia which is com
monly called the angry tree. It reaches the
height of eighty feet after a rapid growth,
and somewhat resembles the century plant.
One of these curious plants was brought from
Australia and set out at Virginia, Nev.,
where it has beep seen by many persons.
When the sun sets the leaves fold up aud the
tender twigs coil tightly, like a little pig’s
tail. If the shoots are handled the leaves
rustle and move uneasily for a time. If this
queer plant is removed from one pot to an
other it seems angry, and the leaves stand
out in all directions like quills on a porcu
pine. A most pungeut aud sickening odor,
said to reseirible that given off by rattle
snakes when annoyed, fills the air, and it is
only after an hour or so that the leaves fold
in the natural way.—Chicago Mail.
A lloyN Essay on Dreatlilng.
A Kentucky school boy, of 13 years, recent
ly wrote the following essay on breathing;
“We breathe with our lungs, our lights, our
kidneys and our livers. If it wasn’t for our
breath we would die when we slept. Our
breath keeps the life agoing through the nose
when we are asleep. Boys who stay in a
room all day should not breathe. They
should wait until they get out iu the fresh
air. Boys in a room make bad air called
carbonicide. CsrhooioklsM sspoimii wimiI
dogs. A lot of soldiers were once in a black
hole in Calcutta and carbonicide got in there
and killed them. Girls sometimes ruin the
breath with corsets that squeeze the diagram.
A big diagram is best for the right kind of
breathing.”
Satan as a Gentleman.
A friend who has great faith in the cate
chism, and teaches it with a pertinacity which
would challenge the admiration of Calvin
himself, is also very particular in instructing
his children to speak politely on all occasions.
The result of his teachings is sometimes amus
ing. This was the case one day when he was
putting the youngest of four through his pre
paratory course. The question was asked,
“Who tempted Eve?"
The little fellow, after a moment’s thought,
with an air of confidence replied: "It's the,
gentleman who lives In hell; I’ve forgotten
his name."—Lewiston Journal.
The Boreeihoe’s Powers as a Mascot.
Among the odds and ends of a somewhat
famous junk shop ander Essex market build
ing are some thousands of horseshoes. They
are not sold, however, to either blacksmiths
or iron founders, but are bought eagerly at
ten cents apiece by Bast aiders, who still have
faith in the horseshoe’s powers os a masoot.
A great many East tide shopkeepers have
horseshoes snugly hid away In their money
drawers. Many a ebeap apartment in the
tenement house region has a gilded horseshoe
over the hall door.—New York Letter.
A Miner’s Luck.
They tell a story of one California man
who, after worlSg in one place for years,
suddenly conceived the idea of digging un
der a huge rock, over which a stream of
clear water was falling, where he waste the
habit of slaking his thirst, and after digging
a sort of tunnel six or eight feet inward, ha
was awarded by the discovery of a pocket of
nuggets from which he took out #68^)00 worth
of gold.—St. Louis Globe-Democrat.
Looking Ahead.
Chinese parents excuse themselves for kill
ing their children by saying that if every one
was allowed to live there wouldn’t be enough
shoemakers to supply them with shoes, and
that many would have to go barefooted in
winter, and thus suffer great discomfort
When a Chinaman can't see through a stone
wall no one else will try.—Detroit Free Press.
UM of High Pressure Steam.
The fact is stated as a sign of the modern
tendency in the construction of marine en
gines that folly one half of the new steamers
built in England have triple expansion en
gines, worked with steam, at the nominal
pressure of 160 pounds—that is, the valves
are set to blow off at that pressure—not be
fore.—New York Commercial Advertiser.
He Saw Finally.
“It costs money to live."
“You bet. Why, I paid $3 the other day
for an umbrella and fifty for an overcoat.
Now the umbrella is used up and the over
coat is wornout."
“Why, how did you—Oh! Ahl Yes.”—
Chatter.
Victoria, Australia.
During the last seven years the land under
tillage in Victoria, Australia, has increased
by 1,000,000 acres, and the ratable value of
country lands has increased from £55,000,000
to £95,000,000 sterling.—Chicago Times.
Borax for tlie Scalp.
One of the best things to cleanse the scalp
• thoroughly is to dissolve one-half teaspoonful
’ of borax in a quai* of water and apply it,
rubbing it in well. Rinse thoroughly in
clear water.—Good Housekeeping.

SATURDAY, AUGUST 20, 1859.
Exeter Hall mod Slare tabor Black
Republicans and the Coolie Trade.
The abolition of eUverj in the British West
India poesesBiona baa demonstrated two inoet
Important facts, to-wit: 1st Free negro latwr
cannot be depended open fur the cultivation
of the soil, because that class of persons are
lazy, indolent and improvident, ud refuse to
labor except npon physical compulsion: 2nd.
White labor cannot be depended upon for
the cultivation of the soil in the warm South
ern latitudes, because white men are physi

Honor to Lieutenant Maury.
The Washington papers state that the Em
peror of France, a few weeks since, says the
Memphis Appeal, caused to be transmitted to
Lieutenant Maury a gold medal, bearing on
one side the Emperor's effigy, and on the
other an inscription highly honorable to the
Lieutenant, This distinguished officer of our
navy has just received from the King of Portu
gal an autograph letter, conferring upon him
the degree of officer of the Tower and Sword,
together with the beautiful insignia of the
order. Lieutenant Maury's countrymen will
bo pleased to learn that tokens of royal ad
miration and regard, either medals or decora
tions, have been offered to him by nearly
every sovereign of Europe, in testimony of
the high estimate set upon his scientific at
tainments, and the value of his labors re
specting wind aud weather nt sea, which
have resulted so beneficially to the naviga
tor aud the merchant, in shortening the
voyages between distant parts of the world. -The
Paris Moniteur of July 14, 1859, says :
At Washington it is. they say. in agitation
to place Lieutenant Maury, the illustrious Di
rector of the National Observatory, in active
service, as without doing so, his promotiou
would, aceording to the regulation of the ser
vice, be impossible. To withdraw sueh a
man from bis scientific labors, in the present
epoch, seems to us more than a fault. Pur-
tug the many years which be has passed on
shore, Maury Las rendered to his country
services which he could not have performed
in distant places, or in the most famous naval
combats. His lals rs, now known to the
whol world, have made an immense step iu
naval science; nori is there either a sailor
or a savant, who, if he were consulted, would
not award to him the highest grades of his
corps. -
Maury, like Humboldt, is one of those men
whose leisure ought to be requested, be
cause it is important as well to science as hu
manity. Let us hope then that the American Govern
ment which contains so many good Captains
in its Navy, shall not Uud it necessary to
take this illustrious man from his noble
latMirs to cover him with honors, which, what
ever they may be, must be always iuferior to
the great services which he has already
rendered.

cess; that he went to Texas oil a visit.vas
eb cted a member of the convention to form

j ction of the constitution by Sao t a .Anna;

cessful termination at the battle of San Ja

A special dispatch from Kansas City con
tains alarming intelligence. AIout 5.000 In
dian warriors of the Raw. Osage and Ca-
manche tribes, have recently removed from
the Little Arkansas to Butler and Greenwood
counties, Kansas Territory, and have been
collecting for sometime for the put pose of
driving back the settlers from the houtier.
The intention is by some supposed to be re
venge for the hanging of two Kaw Indians
at Council Grove some weeks since, but this
seems bardly plausible, us the point of attack
is some seventy miles distaut. I Lis, however,
may be a plea to decoy the men away from
the Grove, so the place may tail an easy
prey Certain it is, that the people oi tuai
place have for some time anticipated an at
tack. On the morning of the 11th a messen
ger arrived at Emporia with information that
an engagement had taken place between the
Indians and whites near Ihe town of Eldorado,
in which one hundred Indians and five whites
were killed. The same day another messen
ger arrived from Chelsea, filteen miles further
up the Whitewater river, bringing a cote
lrom N. D. Storrs, stating that the town was
surrounded by Indians, and 'hat the settlers
were momentarily expecting an attack, and
asking for assistance. A company, ot fifty
left Emporia that evening, expecting to
march to Chelsea, fifty miles dietaut, the next
moruing.

A FIELD OF ROMANCE
British West Indies a Para
dise for Coolies.
CASE OF RAM SAL
ROSE FROM DEEPEST POVERTY TO
BE A lILLIONAIBE.
A Eajah's Son Who Turned Laborer
Bam Singh's World-Wide
Search.
Written for The Evening Star.
"This." recently remarked Joseph Cham
berlain. England's colonial secretary, as
he placed a finger on a map showing Brit
ain's possessions in the West Indies, "is the
paradise of the Hindoo coolie."
"And there, too." added one of his hear
ers, "those same orientals have created an
atmosphere that fairly reeks with ro
mance."
The American or European who has spent
sufficient time in Jamaica or Trinidad to
become acquainted with the ways of the
thousands of Hindoo laborers on the plan
tations and the coolie tradesmen in the
towns invariably makes the same asser
tions. To prove the first, he usually quotes
the case of Ram Sal. and, as evidence of
the latter, he tells the story of a rajah's
son who turned coolie for love's sake.
The Story of 1arn Sal.
Thirty years ago a Hindoo of low caste,
Ram Sal by name, living in a plateau town
of India, found himself inextricably in debt
to a bunnia-a usurious money lender. He
had been bound in his boyhood for the debt.
"7 4i/ '

of his grandfather, and, after working for
nearly twenty years to clear it, he learned
to his distress that, instead of becomingt
smaller, the obligation had steadily in
creased through all the years, owing to his c
insignificant pay of scarcely two pennies a I
day and the excessive rate of interest.
Ram Sal saw starvation staring himself r

The shin that Ram Sal and his family
were huddled on bore ' them in course of
:lme. to Trinidad, and there, by the terms
>f the contraet, Ram Sal wa given quar
ers in a long, lo*5 coeMe baracks, from
which he went out in the morlings to toll
