intil sundown in the coffee and sugar
lelds, the banana, orange and spice groves.
Thus he worked for six years, the-planter
n the meantime feeding him oj rice and
lothing him. and the agent of the bunIa"
eeing to it that until every gant pf Ram's.
ndebtedness was paid not a cent of his hire
-eached. his pocket, but was turned over.,
natead to the agent by Ram's employer.
The Vow of a Coolie.
At the end of the sixth year Ra* '9l felt
be jingle of coin in his hands, fo.r the first
:ime, for the debt of a few rupees that his
grandfather had contracted When he tooi
Ram'S grandmother to wife wq. vq ped out.
t'he ring of the coins stirred up a strange
lesire in the coolie's heart-aitawakened4
his ambition, and he towed then knd there
:hat he would be rich sonie' day and a
)lanter himself. -
To that goal, during the remaining two
years of his identure, Ram Sal saved every
*enny that he earned, and his children, by
heir labor in the fields,-helped increase the
unount. At the end of that time it was
3itiably small-something less than a half
housand dollars-but it. was more mogey
han Ram Sal had ever owned before., and,
what was more important, it was sufficient
or the execution of his present purpose,
he establishment of himself as a silver
;mith in the Hindoo quarter of Port-of
3pain.
Ram Sal had worked at the trade in India
ind was skilled in its ways, so before long
)usiness was plentitul with him and he was
ashioning bracelets and anklets and neck
aces and torques of silver, and sometimes
>f gold, for the women of the better class
)f Hindoos, whose custom it is to use their
women as. walking banks for their surplus.
:n short, 'Ram prospered exceedingly, so
nuch so that at the end of five years he
was able to go into the interior and buy a
'un-down plantation adjoining the one on
which he had been bound for eight years.
Barn Sal, Mllnaire.
In the interim Ram had not forgotten
kow fields were made to yield their in
rease. As a planter he so directed the la
ors of the hundred or more coolies Inden
ured to him that his plantation soon be
'ame noted as one of the most productive
a the valley.
Ram Sal was now fairly started on his
oad to fortune, and each succeeding sttp
ARDEN
AN OF LIES
gainst such odds, and beside whom all
ther men seem to me dwarfed and petty.
There can be no harm in writing of these
natters now. Denis Mallory is very far
way beyond the stir and murmur of this
restern world of ours, and She is far away,
oo. MacKenzie is back in London. He
has an office in Harley street where you
nay consult him at certain hours-for a
:ertain number of guineas. I see him now
.nd then, but we never talk of Paris. Von
Lltdorf is, as I have said, busied in Vienna.
.nd the prince Is with his fathers-od
est his soul!
To no one beyond these few could the
elling of my tale be of consequence. The
hings that stirred us all so deeply never
eached the great public, though heaven
nows they came near enough to reaching
t. The very realm over which we plotted
nd schemed and fought has been quietly
rased from the map of Europe by that
wift and noiseless hand outstretched from
he white throne In the north, which will,
omne day, as quietly, erase the names of
til the remaining little states that we call
he Balkans and of whose inner history we
Enow so little.
But let me come to my tale without fur
her parley save In the matter of this. In
what I shall tell there must be, perforce.
nuch which I did not personally see or
iear. Such things I have gained In part
'romn those others who knew directly of
hem, piartly from my knowledge of what
uch a one would do or say when so placed.
must put myself in the position of the
writer of fanciful tales, who pretends to
ee what is in the minds and hearts of the
mppets his hand dangles. AlU this for a
treater smoothness in the telling and a
ieider scope of view to' him who reads.
I mind that I had been dining that spring
~vening os er on the proper side of the
Iver In the Avenue Kieber, ndthat it
was somewhere near 11 o'clckwhen my
lacre, homeward bound, relied smoothly up
he long straight reach of the Rue de
Kennes.
I have said that it was spring. There.
was a soft, warm little bree.,e that bore
lown the street ad pressed against my
'ace. There were odors abroad, the night
was full of them, the scent of green things
waking to lifE, of the little sticky chestnut
)uds that werd just pushing out from
ranch and twig-they would be great
'ones of fragrant blossom, in a fortnight;
sf starting leaves and quickened sod, and
hverywhere the heavy fragranoe of lilacs
1ewly in flower. It stirred the blood, sent
t mounting like wine to one's had, set
mne's heart to jumping, filled one with a
rague unrest.
"The world's too tame," qald L, taing a
ong breath and throwing out my arms,.
'There's no romance left in It-cot eves
iere in romantic Paris. Who could be re
nantic In Jan opera hat and a dress coat?
)ne needs silk breeches and shoe buckles
mud a wig and a sword." I aIsed any -sgcr
ad regarded it with disfavor. "Fancy
:arrying about a silly lile bit of wood
Ike that!" said I, mormely. "What's the
rood of,it? It's nt even ornameal,= and

a dead. I'd Uhe to fght a duel or-o
>verthrow a kinm er run of with some
hody's beautiful bride .wrb had fallen in
eve with me at sight, but I can't, just
>eeause it's the meU.n century and
aecause tis things a stick and not a

Iboran was the daughter of Chundar Lal,
Df a plateau village of India. In the same
town Ram Singh lived, and Ram was en
gaged to marry -Iboran when he could se
cured rupees enough to make the wedding
festivities. To that end he weit to work
for an Englishman In a distant town. Two
years later he returned to his own village
with .the money, only to find that Chundar
Lal and his daughter had followed a coolie
contractor across the "black water."
Ram Singh was In despair. To his ques
tion as to where Iboran had gone no one
could definitely answer him, not even the
contractors, who had sent the girl along
with hundreds of others to the seacoast to
be shipped to any one of a half dosen places
-Fiji, Natal, Trinidad, Jamaica, British
auiana, Montserrat.
Ram Singh vowed that great as was the
task before him be would find Iboran and
marry her. There was but one way to do
that. She had become a coolie; he must
become a coolie also.
"Perhaps she has been sent to Fiji," the
contraetora told him. So Ram Singh in
lentured himself for a term of six years as
1 coolie on a Fiji plantation.
Arrived there he set about hunting for
[boran when he was not required to be in
the fields. But his search was in vain-she
was not In Fiji.
For six years Ram Singh worked and
waited. Then "Maybe she is in Natal," the
plantation owner, who knew his story, told
him. And straightway Ram Singh bound
himself for a term of six years to a Natal
planter.
The End of the Lover's Travels.
Again It was six years wasted, six years
f working and waiting. But on the day
that his contract was ended Ram Singh
signed away his liberty for a third term of
six years and was taken in a coolie ship to
rrinidad.
Here he got his first ray of hope. One
lay as he was about his search he ran
cross a man who had been a neighbor of
1hundar Lal, Iboran's father, and he told
Ram Singh that Iboran, mourning for her
over, whom she never expected to see
gain, had gone with her father from In
Ila to Jamaica.
That was enough to cause the years nec
sssary to his release to pass swiftly for
Ram Singh, and then with the money that
he had saved, he took passage to Jainaica
ind began his search with renewed heart.
It was in an Interior village that Iboran
was found, still unmarried, still true to the
nemory of her lover. At first she would
iot believe that Ram Singh was other than
i ghost, but when he had convinced her

way planned.

As we were very anxious to wind up om
experience of the coronation festivities 01
King Edward with those in Calcutta, we
left Agra rather earlier than we had ex
pected to. and after a very tiring journey o1
twenty-eight or thirty hours arrived a1
the Howrah station across the river from
the great city Itself. In spite of all we had
read of Calcutta we were surprised at the
width of the River Hooghly, which is over
half a mile wide in front of the town and
is a beautiful sight, filled with shipping of
all sorts, for the largest ships, even the
P. & O. steamers, come up as far as the
city.
Our first impressions are supposed to be
the most truthful, so I may say that our
first was of a very "western" city with a
very eastern population. The houses and
public buildings are most imposing, the
private houses being much alike, very
large, square and of plaster-covered brick,
set in beautiful gardens,- surrounded
by high walls. As the feeding of the poor
of Calcutta was to take place at 2 o'clock
on the 27th, the day after our arrival, we
could do little else on the morning of that
day except drive about the town to get an
idea of its extent and appearance, and we
found there the beautiful river along
which Calcutta is built has on one side of
it a very fine drive, where the "wit and
fashion" of the city takes the air of an
evening, and which runs from Eden Gar
dens, past the fine old fort, where the com
mander-in-chief lives, up to the lieutenant
governor's beautiful palace, "lielvidere,"
which is much finer than Government
House. On the other side of the drive is
the Maidan or plain, a great open field
dotted with trees and ponds, where one can
walk with fairly good turf under one's feet
or have a morning canter, if one prefers.
This splendid "breathing space" - for Cal
cutta is a mile and a half in length and has
a very good drive also on its other side, and
at one end the race track, which is a gay
scene in the morning between 7 and 9, for
all of those who ride, to a man, arise, win
ter and summer, at 6:30 and sally forth for
an hour's exercise on the track.
When the City Poor Eat.
The feeding of the city poor we found a
most interesting sight. Between forty and
forty-five thousand Hindoos and Mahomme
dans, principally beggars and scavengers,
but also gharl drivers and khalassigor or
boatmen, were fed. All attention possible
was paid to caste, and all the expense
possible borne by one man, a rich Hindoo,
the Babu Byujan Lall Lohia, at least all
save the meat for the' Mohammedans who,
for some religious reason, could not be pro
vided by the Hindoo. On dit that he wishes
to be made a knight. What more laudable!
A part of the Maldan, large enough to
seat the forty-five thousand, was fenced in
with native mats, and then divided up into
blocks 225 by seventy-five feet, which would
each accommodate one thousand people,
seated on the ground. Twenty of the en
closures were reserved for the Hindoos
slow mind. Yes, there is more to come.

her head to play a momentous trick, plays
1 -a,t tho.- urhly to the end. She's a
good sportswoman, Fate, I've seen her
play strange games. Aye, and I've taken
a hand with her at times, but I've never
seen her yet in so wholly wanton a mood
as this.

How the Coolie ret to America.
The "elder" does the business for him.
At the door of the apartment used by
the elder as an offle he is told to enter
hy a policeman whose business it is to
guard the pernon of the eldar and who
stands eyeing the coolie from the door
way during the entire interview. The
coolie's first business is to produce his
ctsh and say that he wishes for some in
formation, which the spirit of his an
cestors, through a medium, has told him
that the elder possessed. The sight of
the cash, together with the compliment
which the ancestors have seen fit to pay
the elder, cm scarcely fail to make him
good-natured and accommodating, and
he declares in the politest terms his
readiness to serve the coolie to the fullest
extent of his power. The oolie then
makes known his errand, by speiking
of his poverty and the prospect there is
of riches in America, and aski the elder
to show him a way to procure a ticket.
The elder of course know all about it.
He has had raanv cuses before, and some
of them he Ins since learned have gotten
rich. Has the coolie a family ? and if
so, has he any robnst, healthy girls, or
well-behaved, industrious sons of a sala
ble age ? The coolie thinks that he has,
Well, then it can easilv be arranged.
(live security on them and borrow the
money. But who has the money to lend?
The brokers ir Hong Kong, of course.
Hut how is the coolie to rivch them ? O.
the elder will arran?e all that, and will
get the ticket for the coolie just as soon
as he can draw up and execute a bond on
the coolie's family. The coolie is over
loved. How much wi 11 a ticket cost ?
The elder cannot tell until he gets it.
There will be some eot besides the price
of the ticket, but it will mike little or no
difference to the coolie, wlyi can earn so
much when he gets to America. When
the time for the execution of the bond
has been fixed for some dav in the sub
sequent week, and the grateful coolie has
expressed his thanks, the elder escorts
him out bv the sentinel with a word of
caution about expressing his intentions
in the presence of government officials.
Straightwav a swift footed messenger
leaves the offices of the mandarin, armed
with a bundle of pape rs, and nnder in
struction to proceed at his liveliest pace
to the distant city of Canton. He is a
government messenger, recognized by
his dress and gait ; and toll-gates open,
ferryboats hasten, pedestrians give him
room, and sedan-chairs turn out, that he
may be able to give his important de
spatches into the hands of the ticket
broker in Canton. This in due time is
faithfully done, for a fee has been prom
ised him upon his return. The broker
finds in the bundle of papers an order
for a ticket to San Francisco, for which
the mandarin promises to pay, with a
high rate of interest, in one year. There
being no better security than a manda
rin's word, the ticket, or the broker's
promise to give it npon the application
of the coolie, is properlv made out and
given to the messenger, while the man
darin's letter is carefully filed away and
marked "payable one year from date,
fortv dollars, with broker's fee, ten dol
lars and interest." Upon the return of
the messenger, the mandarin adds fiftv
per cent, to the fifty dollars which he is
now honnd to pay the broker, and sends
a note for "seventy-five dollars with in
terest pnynble ten months from date,"
risking the signature of the elder. To
this note the elder signs his name, and
shortly after receives the broker's ticket.
The next transaction is by bond, and
n lawyer must be called to draft one.
These semi-learned pettifoggers are as
plenty in China, as in America, and are
as prompt to respond to the call of the
fee. Therefore it requires hut a short,
time to write a bond, by means of which
the coolie acknowledges an indebtedness
to the elder of one hundred dollars with
interest, and to secure the pnvment of
which he pledges the persons of his wife,
his three sons, and his two daughters.
If he should fail to pny the amount in
nine months from (late, either at the
Chinese banking-house named in San
Francisco, or at the office of the mort
gagee, then the family maybe sold forth
with ; together, if they will bring money
enough to cancel the debt, and singly,
if no one person can be found to buy
them all at a reasonable price. This
done, the coolie is sent for. He has no
knowledge of the first cost of the ticket,
and if he expresses astonishment, upon
his arrival, that the price is so high, he
is told thnt with thirty dollars a month
in America he can pny it iii less thnn half
the time named in the bond. I !! hesi
tatingly he then signs his name, or in
structs another person to do an, takes
the ticket, and scarcely dreaming that a
failure of payment is possible, returns as
gaily ns before to his family to rejoice
with them in the delightful prospect
which the possession of till . '"kef gives
th(n all, nnd to prepare for his long and
perilous journey.

The Horse from a Moral Standpoint, j
The driver who fights his horses has
not got through the first lesson in the
mtnagemeut of these anmils. Some
strike their horses in a fit of anger ;
others beat them as a punishment for
what thev concsive to 1)9 sinful acts on
the part of th9 hor-ie. Now, the fact is,
the horse never does wrong on purpose.
In this respect he is better than most
men. If h3 refuses to pull it is the fault
of educition, and if he runs awiv and
kicks things to pieces, it is because he is
frightened. Men, when they are scared,
do some very foolish things, and they are
excused ; but no allowance is rntde for
the runawty hor-w. He is kiekel, and
cuffed, and beat, as if what was done
wivs on purpose to hurt somibodv, and
not in view of supposed dinger, which
the poor animal w is trying to flss from.
Horses never kick without a motive.
They use their heels for defence, and the
first kick at the trace is givan tn wrd
off what to them seems danger. No
horse ever kicked for any oth?r purpose.
They cheerish no ill feeling against any
one, and alwiys do as far as they know
what is right. All horses can be educa
ted to do whatever the driver wishes.
They obey cheerfully and without grum
bling, even though put to rest with half
rations in a cold, filthy, muddy stable.
A horse can easilv be taught to know
the harness will not hnirt him. Then he
will not kick at all or run away. He is
also easily taught to pull by the traces or
by the halter strap. If yon want him to
pull well on the halter," all you have to
do is to hitch him with something he can
easily break, and he will soon learn to
pull back with such fore? that no bridle
will hold him. If you want him to pull
well in the traces, give hira a light load
until he lenrns to move it, and he will
soon pull his bst at heavv loads. Hor
ses balk or pull just as they are taught.
It does not matter which end of the
horse vou fasten the weight to. Thev
will balk as readily when hitehpd by the
traces, if improperly trained, as wdien
hitched bv the halter ; and thev will
pnll hack as faithfully by the halter, if
trained to do so, as thev pnll forward.
when hitched bv the traces to a wagon.
It is an easy matter to teach a horse to
refuse to pull at either the halter or
traces, and it is equally as easy to teach
him to pull by the same means. If those
who drive horses would keep this fact
in view, nnd keep their temper out of
view, they would never be guilty of the
shameful act of beating their team in a
mnd hole or on the hillside.

1-)are, and of immense size. Now I was
curious, sure, and resolved to lay for the

Two hours or more I sat there and won
dered if the owner of the feet would come

the spot where I saw the track was on my

tion that my nttention was mostly direct
ed, thinking the visitor would appear

(tod !" as I saw the object of my solitude

Their only object in visiting my camp
